caveats: a: The occupation source is secondary and covers all firm sizes.
a: Occupation shares are headcount, not payroll, and do not isolate independent targets.
a: The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio uses gross commodity receipts, so even genuine labor savings are structurally small relative to sales.
rho: No grain-merchant-specific five-year AI implementation study was found.
rho: One harvest season is insufficient to assess reliability across commodity cycles.
e: USDA counted 312 grain-and-oilseed cooperatives in 2024 but does not state their share of 424510 LMM firms.
e: Some cooperatives can merge or sell assets even though ordinary third-party control acquisition is unavailable.
e: The supplied firm count is an estimate based on a broad distributor margin bridge that may poorly represent thin-margin grain merchants.
s5: The Gallup survey is not specific to grain merchants, rural firms, or the LMM band.
s5: Survey intentions include transfers that may not qualify or close.
q: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in grain merchandising.
q: Local storage scarcity and crop conditions can dominate pricing power.
q: The estimate excludes volume effects and implementation difficulty.
d5: Acreage is an imperfect proxy for handled volume.
d5: Five-year outcomes are highly sensitive to weather, yields, trade policy, and biofuel demand.
d5: The source includes cotton and rice in addition to the main 424510 crops.
o: The necessary operator may be integrated, cooperative, or public rather than an independent LMM merchant.
o: Official inspection is not required for every domestic movement, though measurement and quality control remain necessary.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring commodity origination and resale earning merchandising or basis margins, often combined with receiving, weighing, grading coordination, drying, storage, blending, hedging, and loading services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default lens is narrowed to independently controllable merchant wholesalers because farmer-member cooperatives and captive agribusiness locations have governance and control-transfer mechanics that are not coherent with conventional firm acquisition; the operating activities remain otherwise unchanged.
lens.excluded_activities: Farmer-member cooperatives not available for a conventional qualifying control sale, captive or vertically integrated units of large agribusinesses, pure brokers, standalone storage businesses not primarily wholesaling, farms, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independently controllable merchant wholesalers repeatedly buying and reselling corn, soybeans, wheat, other grains, and field beans to external processors, exporters, feed users, and other trade customers, including country or terminal elevators operated primarily for wholesaling.

a.bridge: The industry occupation source reports headcount rather than wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure: 16% office/administrative support, 9% sales, 6% management, 16% farming occupations, 6% production, and 35% transportation/material moving. I assign substantial exposure to contracting, document entry, market synthesis, customer communication, reconciliation, and scheduling, with low exposure to sampling, physical handling, maintenance, and transport. Wage weighting raises the contribution of management and commercial roles, but existing automatic weighing and control systems reduce remaining exposure, creating two-sided error.
caveats: The occupation source is secondary and covers all firm sizes.
Occupation shares are headcount, not payroll, and do not isolate independent targets.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio uses gross commodity receipts, so even genuine labor savings are structurally small relative to sales.
a.change_evidence: Current payroll-weighted SOC and O*NET task data from independent grain merchants, combined with application logs identifying work already handled by trading, elevator-control, scale, and settlement systems.
a.rationale: AI can assist bid and contract preparation, market and basis analysis, producer communications, settlement reconciliation, logistics scheduling, quality-document review, and compliance records. The majority physical workforce and already automated scale and conveyor controls sharply limit exposure across total payroll.

d5.bridge: USDA projects planted acreage for eight major row crops to decline 2.4% from 2026/27 to 2035/36, with corn acreage easing, soybeans slipping after an initial rise, and wheat flat. Acreage is not merchant-service quantity: yield, inventory turns, exports, domestic crushing and feed use, and channel share can move handled bushels differently. I translate the stable-to-soft acreage outlook into approximately flat five-year service demand, allowing yield and trade growth to raise throughput or direct and integrated channels to lower it.
caveats: Acreage is an imperfect proxy for handled volume.
Five-year outcomes are highly sensitive to weather, yields, trade policy, and biofuel demand.
The source includes cotton and rice in addition to the main 424510 crops.
d5.change_evidence: USDA baseline bushel-level production, domestic-use, stocks, crush, feed, ethanol, and export projections by commodity, combined with Census and state data on the independent merchant share of throughput.
d5.rationale: Grain and bean use for food, feed, fuel, processing, and export remains large, but USDA's current baseline does not indicate broad acreage expansion. Productivity can sustain bushels on fewer acres, while trade policy, biofuel rules, weather, and processor integration create material variance.

caveats: USDA counted 312 grain-and-oilseed cooperatives in 2024 but does not state their share of 424510 LMM firms.
Some cooperatives can merge or sell assets even though ordinary third-party control acquisition is unavailable.
The supplied firm count is an estimate based on a broad distributor margin bridge that may poorly represent thin-margin grain merchants.
e.change_evidence: A full ownership and governance classification of the supplied 424510 firm universe using USDA cooperative records, state elevator licenses, corporate linkage data, and confidential financials, followed by verification of normalized EBITDA and recurring external trade.
e.rationale: A meaningful part of the supplied firm population should be recurring external merchant businesses, but grain marketing has a large cooperative presence and also includes facilities controlled by integrated agribusiness groups. Those structures are not conventionally transferable control targets, while some nominal merchants are too owner-dependent or lack durable origination relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424510 ($3,068,013,463) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424510 ($274,177,367,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $5,825,504 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424510); class <5: 645 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,237,922 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $481,852 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 353 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,766,144 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,906,311 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 302 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,418,560 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,685,990 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 352 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,504,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,176,654 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $396,851,726 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,122,041 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,778,093,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $315,869,212 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 655

caveats: The necessary operator may be integrated, cooperative, or public rather than an independent LMM merchant.
Official inspection is not required for every domestic movement, though measurement and quality control remain necessary.
o.change_evidence: Commodity-specific flow studies quantifying farm-direct delivery, on-farm storage, processor integration, cooperative handling, electronic-market transactions, and independent merchant throughput, plus interviews with processors, exporters, and producers.
o.rationale: Year-five grain flows still require accountable operators to take title or custody, manage credit and hedges, receive and store bulk product, control commingling, coordinate grading and weighing, and load trucks, railcars, barges, or ships. Software can automate matching and administration, and some farmers or processors can contract directly, but it cannot eliminate physical custody, certified measurements, quality accountability, and logistics execution.

caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in grain merchandising.
Local storage scarcity and crop conditions can dominate pricing power.
The estimate excludes volume effects and implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Contract-, bid-, and lot-level data linking measured cost reductions to basis, storage and handling fees, producer retention, buyer terms, and realized gross margin through several crop and renewal cycles.
q.rationale: Implemented savings initially improve the operator's merchandising margin, but commodity benchmarks, visible local bids, producer choice, processor and exporter bargaining, and highly competitive transport routes should pass much of the gain through basis or service terms. Local origination density, scarce storage, quality blending, logistics execution, and trust can preserve a minority of the benefit.

caveats: No grain-merchant-specific five-year AI implementation study was found.
One harvest season is insufficient to assess reliability across commodity cycles.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at independent grain merchants measuring hours per contract and settlement, reconciliation exceptions, hedge mismatches, demurrage, grade disputes, control failures, and avoided hiring over multiple crop cycles.
rho.rationale: Commercial copilots, document extraction, reconciliation, and logistics planning are feasible within five years, but implementation must integrate with commodity trading, hedging, grading, scale, inventory, and settlement systems. Financial exposure from quantity, quality, timing, or hedge errors and the legal weight of official records keep humans in approval loops.

s5.bridge: Gallup measured five-year intentions across U.S. employer-business owners, 22% of whom planned to sell or transfer ownership in fall 2024. I apply that rate only after excluding nonconventional cooperative and captive structures through eligibility, then reduce the center for plans that do not close or do not create a qualifying change of control. Rural owner age could create upward error, while cyclical economics and family or strategic continuity could create downward error.
caveats: The Gallup survey is not specific to grain merchants, rural firms, or the LMM band.
Survey intentions include transfers that may not qualify or close.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 424510 control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, state elevator-license changes, cooperative and agribusiness announcements, lenders, and rural M&A advisers, deduplicated against a validated eligible-firm cohort.
s5.rationale: Independent country merchants can transfer, but intended sales do not all execute and no detailed-industry closed-deal denominator was found. Strategic consolidation supports some transfers, while family succession, asset-only closures, and deferral reduce qualifying events.

principal_driver: Physical custody, quality accountability, and transport coordination remain indispensable even as commercial workflows become more automated.
principal_weakness: Very low labor intensity and a large cooperative or captive footprint limit both savings and conventional acquisition availability.

sources.supports: Describes uniform official grain inspection and weighing, authorized service providers, official certificates, quality attributes, equipment verification, and appeal processes.

sources.supports: Shows that modern official loose-grain weighing uses load cells and a scale-control computer to automate gates, readings, tare, gross, and net weights.

sources.supports: USDA counted 1,620 agricultural cooperatives in 2024, including 312 whose major commodity was grain and oilseeds; it describes member ownership, mergers, locations, and services.

sources.supports: States that U.S. grain moves to domestic and foreign markets through truck, rail, and barge in a highly competitive transportation market.
sources.title: Transportation of U.S. Grains: A Modal Share Analysis

sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and separately measured closure and uncertainty.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Explains that trade-industry output prices are margins received by wholesalers and retailers and illustrates their sensitivity to small selling-price changes.
